What is humanistic about data visualisation? As a body of methods for exploring and explicating quantitative datasets, data visualisation has grown in importance in the humanities with the rise of the digital age and, with it, the age of “big data”; yet visualisation still has the air of an alien element. Its growing prominence challenges us to rethink the relationship between traditional literary analysis and the new forms of analysis and evidence that computing technologies make available. It also challenges us to consider how we might square traditional criteria for rigorous analysis in the humanities with digital methods of analysis. How do literary keywords such as style, influence and genre demand rethinking in the context of quantitative analysis? Do we necessarily abandon a theoretical commitment to foregrounding the material basis of texts when we use digital methods of “distant reading” to understand textual transmission? What is the relationship in literary studies—which continues to give a special place, in catalogues, syllabi, anthologies and indeed literary databases, to the individual author and the mythos of genius—between data and the data point? In English Studies, a field defined by its attention to verbal phenomena, how can visual tools and practices rework, rather than simply refigure, verbal information? How can the distinctive textures of experience that humanistic documents—such as poems, plays or letters—record complicate the codes, frameworks and methods of visualisation techniques that arose in the context of business and the sciences? Will the imbrication of digital methods into established humanities disciplines affect their social paradigms? When we use visual graphics to translate numerical data, what do we lose by privileging visual over other means of encoding information?

For researchers who seek to incorporate digital methods into their work, for teachers who seek to bring digital methods into the curriculum and for administrators who seek guidelines for evaluating digital humanities projects, such questions get to the heart of an urgent problem in the digital humanities: reconciling the young age of data analysis with the rich texture of research that humanities scholarship emphasises. An important feature that separates the humanities from the sciences is that while the scientist’s methods can be paraphrased without any loss, in the humanities the description itself is understood to be part of the method. (The anthropologist Clifford Geertz captured an aspect of this praxis in the phrase “thick description”, referring to the careful negotiation of the researcher’s frameworks of observation.)[Footnote1](#)

For humanists, part of the challenge of honouring this praxis while using digital methods is a problem of design. As we build digital systems to manage new volumes and configurations of data relevant to humanists, the careful negotiation of frameworks persists, albeit at layers that are sometimes invisible to the user; the values of the discipline require us to find ways to make those invisible layers visible. In a 2014 article, Ted Underwood calls on humanities scholars to bring theory and awareness to a set of digital research practices that, precisely because they are so commonplace, have become “invisibly naturalized in our disciplines”: for example, stopping, as a first port of call, at Google, ProQuest or the Literature Online Database, all forms of full-text search that operate according to oblique algorithms and return incomplete responses to most inquiries.[Footnote2](#) Scholars who design digital artifacts themselves are well advised, as the information scholars Alan Galey and Stan Ruecker suggest, to make visible the intellectual frameworks that inform an artifact’s configuration, such as the theories it has reified as models, the trajectory of prior research on which it builds and possible methodological alternatives it has declined.[Footnote3](#) By such means, the artifact’s design, the prototyping equivalent of description, can articulate its theoretical and methodological frameworks.

The other part of the challenge is a literacy problem; when technological givens for some are unknowns for others, even the visible can be illegible. For example, as Clifford Wulfman points out, audiences often believe that data visualisation is a discovery tool, whereas often data visualisation serves instead to refine arguments already made or illustrate conclusions already drawn. “Data visualizations, particularly in the form of word maps and plotted graphs, have become a hallmark of the so-called ‘digital humanities’”, he writes. “It is important for creators and readers of these depictions to remember that they are not ‘data’ but readings, interpretations of data meditated by programmed algorithms and hermeneutic desires.”[Footnote4](#)

As Jefferson Bailey and Lily Pregill note, although data visualisation has been labelled a hot new trend, the practice of creating visual representations of data is ancient. Early modern Bibles used tree diagrams to show the lineage of Christ; Polynesian sailors wove palm-fibre maps that used abstract visual codes to convey navigational information about winds and tides. Nonetheless, only with the rise of modern statistics in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries did the field emerge as a self-conscious body of practice. Many data visualisations from this period focus on health and populations: John Snow used a map overlay to trace the sources of London’s cholera epidemic; Florence Nightingale used sunburst charts to show that “war mortality” included many avoidable diseases; Charles Joseph Minard’s Sankey diagram of the decline of Napoleon’s army during the march on Russia is, today, the most famous data visualisation of all time.[Footnote5](#) In the 1980s, the spread of computer graphics software drew this statistics-oriented field into the culture of computation.[Footnote6](#) In short, the field of data visualisation grew on the trellises of computing and the quantitative sciences—cultures of knowledge production with their own distinctive values, practices and “trading zones” of interdisciplinary exchange.[Footnote7](#) The humanities are a latecomer to the field, and the questions that attended the rise of humanities computing are now being turned to this overlooked disciplinary cousin. In the domain of English Studies, which extends its scope from linguistics to literary studies to philology, the new field reinvigorates some of our oldest questions about the relationship between verbal and visual representation. How should humanists make use of data visualisation? And how does data visualisation make use of the materials of the humanities?

Martin Jay’s classic book on the enduring relationship between knowledge and vision—he reminds us, in the opening pages, of the visual basis of such words as *imagination*, *inspection*, *introspection*, *perception* and *speculation*, an understandable trend given that some thirty percent of the cortex is dedicated to visual processing—ends in the 1980s, just before graphics software brought data visualisation into the public consciousness.[Footnote8](#) Yet his careful examination of the “scopic economies” of other eras in science, philosophy and art—that is, the visual codes on which they rely, and the beliefs about ontology and epistemology that such codes reify: the eternalising Albertian grid, the “visual madness” of Baroque art, the fatal eye of the camera—offers a model for inquiring into the visual regime that governs our own era of data science, data journalism and data-driven policy. The field of practice that has, at its high end, the work of practitioners like Ben Shneiderman, and at its low end the documents on the website Floor Charts—which catalogues the ridiculous poster board props used by US senators to support their arguments, apotheoses of bad visualisation that thereby attest all the more disturbingly to the authority of visualisation—deserves forms of aesthetic appreciation and sceptical hermeneutics in which the humanities has deep experience.[Footnote9](#)

In the process, we will doubtless rewrite some parts of the field’s current history, as well as the histories that the field has helped to write. Consider the diagram of Napoleon’s march into Russia, the one graphic that is certain to grace any public-facing book or talk on “infoviz”. Edward Tufte, a high priest of information design, has commented that it “may well be the best statistical graphic ever drawn”.[Footnote10](#) According to Tufte’s principles, the graphic succeeds, in part, because it gives us the relevant information and no more. The x- and y-axes show the geographic area between Poland and Moscow. A line traces the path of Napoleon’s army as it approaches Moscow, then retreats; the line’s width, which represents the number of soldiers, narrows dramatically as the army progresses. An index at the bottom of the page suggests why: the falling temperature, which reached a low of −30 degrees Celsius. (One commentator writes, “The simple but fascinating temperature line below the viz shows how cold ultimately defeated Napoleon’s army.”)[Footnote11](#) Yet this design lends itself to misreading. Recently, when I put Tufte’s graphic on the projector, one of my students pointed out that temperature was not a direct cause of the deaths in Napoleon’s army; most of his soldiers died of typhus.[Footnote12](#)

Which reopens Napoleon’s march as material for visualisation: what would a graphic of the march that foregrounds the destructive path of typhus look like? It’s true that the falling temperatures may have had an indirect effect, as the fleas that spread typhus may have spread faster as soldiers shared blankets in the cold; but graphs of indirect influences have their own requirements. The questions that arise from this general problem are questions of data analysis: does correlation, in this case, imply causation, and if so how could we find out; how could we trace the path of morbidity as well as mortality; what kinds of additional data would we require—typhus mortality rates, medical data about soldiers, night temperature—and how could we find and analyse it? By such means, solving problems in data visualisation can create pressure on humanists to dig deeper into the operations of data science. The origins of the term *data* notwithstanding, the classics of data visualisation are far from givens.

The articles in this issue advance a unified set of themes. First, they present first-person accounts of visually exploring large datasets associated with literary and cultural history. Claude Willan discusses his use of visual tools to investigate the circulation of manuscript poetry in early modern England. Christopher York discusses his work as the data visualisation specialist on MIT’s Comédie-Française Registers Project (CFRP), which provides resources for exploring and visualising data associated with the history of France’s foremost theatrical company, the Comédie-Française. Susan Schreibman, Vinayak Das Gupta and Neale Rooney explain how visual tools not only illuminated data within Letters of 1916, a “public engagement digital humanities project” that focuses on the circulation of letters in Ireland during a crucial historical year, but also helped the project’s directors to parse metadata concerning the project itself, and thus direct the project to maximise the public’s involvement. In the cases of York, Schreibman, Das Gupta and Rooney, especially, the authors consider the distinction between one’s goals when analysing data firsthand and one’s goals when making data available to the public for analysis and providing tools to that end.

Second, the articles in this issue show how computational methods fold into traditional humanities practice. Writing for *The New Republic*, the historian Anthony Grafton once defended a view of the humanities—*magnus mihi labor incumbit*—as a messy, heroic business: “the great humanists are furious, passionate giants who heave one mountain onto another as they stage their attacks on Olympus”.[Footnote13](#) At first blush, it can be hard to relate these mountains to Silicon Valley in terms of anything but contrasts: the dusty archive versus the clean server, the rule-breaking of human expertise versus the rule-following of expert systems, the Bergsonian *durée* of subjective time versus the clock speed of computer time. Yet although computing entails new information literacies, many computational practices correspond to practices that humanists perform already. What machine learning calls *topic modelling*, for example, is the general equivalent, as a form of exploratory analysis, of a human researcher reading through all of all the texts in a corpus in order to identify discourses and flag anomalies. When the historian Michael Mahoney described computer technologies as having a “protean nature”, this is, in part, what he meant; the history of computing is a human history, since computers can so easily reify, as legacy architectures, the practices and agendas of the human communities that build them.[Footnote14](#) The future of computing is likewise a human future, especially as we improve at modelling digitally the procedures in each of C. P. Snow’s two cultures for exploring complexity, with the aim of turning such procedures, in Willard McCarty’s words, “into algorithmic performances”.[Footnote15](#)

As Willan’s first-person account indicates, his research drew on what database designers call “legacy databases”, including library catalogues, hardbound verse concordances and similar “paper machines”.[Footnote16](#) The materials he sought were difficult to find, in part because the circulation of Jacobite poetry, since it constituted sedition, was secretive, and in part because library finding aids can only rehearse the interests of their creators. Jacobite verses may be hidden in books fat with inventories, receipts and other texts; catalogues may fail to specify that Jacobite verses are Jacobite, instead calling them simply “songs”, “poems” or “political poems”. The goal of gathering data for computer analysis led him to sift through these sources; sifting through them revealed more instances of poems he already knew from his time in various archives. Analysis pulled from the data hidden facets of this secret culture, which visualisation codified for human interpretation: which manuscripts belonged to insiders in the manuscript network; which poems Jacobites liked best, which events inspired the most topical verse, which poems lingered in the minds of the reading public.[Footnote17](#) In this research circuit, Willan says, digital methods were not alternatives to traditional practice, but rather extensions of it: “it was my archival work that led me to digital methods, and digital methods in turn led me back to more focused close reading of materials I discovered in the archive”.[Footnote18](#) Willan’s research shows how data analysis can harmonise with the pleasures and pains of the archive, providing old philology with new maps, new bibliographies, positive feedback loops.

Third, the authors make a tacit case for bringing data literacy into humanities education. York’s discussion of the CFRP analytics tool, which he designed as a research and pedagogical aid, moves through several key concepts in data literacy: exploratory data analysis, confirmatory data analysis, the visual information-seeking mantra. These concepts belong in a humanist’s repertoire, he emphasises:

At first exposure, the tools, techniques and concerns of data science and database analysis seem foreign to the humanities, but they can be taught and learned as a stepwise extension of traditional scholarly scepticism, moving from qualitative sources of evidence to quantitative ones. Data literacy—in the form of fluency with descriptive statistics—often suffices to make nuanced arguments about humanities datasets derived from cultural heritage sources.[Footnote19](#)

Thus a student working with the CFRP tool and dataset might note, with an eye to the statistical distribution of authors,

that 50% of Comédie-Française authors were performed on fewer than 17 evenings. This merely formalises an insight that archival historians might previously have summarised as, “examination of the daily registers reveals the extent to which Moliere, Racine and Corneille dominated the Comédie-Française repertory”. Rising data literacy will enable humanists to walk easily between two such idiomatic formulations, and see them as expressions of similar insights.[Footnote20](#)

With respect to visualisation, specifically, some of the concepts that the CFRP analytics tool teaches students entail an understanding that data visualisation often serves as a tool of explication rather than analysis. Students who learn the difference gain, in the process, a crucial ingredient of twenty-first-century civic literacy: the ability to parse the information graphics that increasingly crowd the newspaper, the web, the Senate floor; to understand their genesis as something other than an omniscient computer output. Visual data literacy is a key skill for citizenship in the digital age, requiring training in both data analysis and the interpretation of visual codes, particularly since some forms of data visualisation, such as tree maps, do not have an intuitive layout. While York’s focus lies with data literacy rather than civic literacy as such, a major aim of the CFRP is to provide an education in information management that can apply equally to the management of drafts of history old and new. Students can use the CFRP dataset and analytics site to learn not only the answers to specific historical questions, but also the kinds of questions that one can usefully ask a dataset of this kind, as well as the fundamentals of data-driven research. (What kinds of research do box graphs best serve? What kinds do network graphs best serve? What is the difference between exploratory and confirmatory analysis?)

Along similar lines, York and his colleagues address second-order questions relating to data analysis—that is, questions concerning the methods and values of the field—that, in their attention to matters of culture, are substantially humanities questions, and which, in helping to make data analysis available to scrutiny, also constitute an important form of data literacy.[Footnote21](#) Digital humanities datasets require foreign expertise, which is to say that their management tends to require collaboration across disciplines, as between humanists who frame a project’s goals and technicians who do the work of modelling, mining and maintaining. Digital humanities datasets are never complete—partly because no dataset is ever complete, but also for reasons specific to data provenance in the field. (Whereas most datasets are purpose-generated by the same institution that consumes the results, as when a government conducts a census for its own uses, humanities data is nearly always of different provenance; historical institutions usually collect such data for almost any reason *but* answering a specific scholar’s research questions. As a result, data science in the humanities contends with problems of incompleteness at the level of the data source that can prevent the kind of synoptic view on which statistical analysis thrives; unless we can be sure the 1890 census has data for all women, we cannot be sure what percentage of twenty-year-old women was unmarried.) And because domain expertises from the humanities shape digital humanities datasets, digital humanities datasets often fail to fit traditional models for managing data, which tend to reflect domain expertises from business or science. (In Lisa Gitelman’s memorable phrase, “Raw data is an oxymoron”.[Footnote22](#))

Fourth, the writers in this issue demonstrate the value of project-driven research.[Footnote23](#) A defining ethos of humanities computing is that “Making things, as a thinking practice, is not only formative but transformative”.[Footnote24](#) The visualisations discussed here participate in that ethos, as they adhere to approaches that use visualisation in research not merely for communication, but for exploration and discovery. Indeed, Stéfan Sinclair, Stan Ruecker and Milena Radzikowska argue that the value of a “visualization system” in the humanities relies precisely on its ability to support exploration:

a visualization that produces a single output for a given body of material is of limited usefulness; a visualization that provides many ways to interact with the data, viewed from different perspectives, is better; a visualization that contributes to new and emergent ways of understanding the material is best.[Footnote25](#)

Of course, the ability of scholars to make effective use of such approaches depends on their exposure to humanities pedagogies that emphasise *production literacies*: the ability to describe, design and use tools in various media as well as understand their functionality.

Moreover, project work often entails transformation by the fact of collaboration. The very basis of Letters of 1916 as a public digital project adds material to the historical record that would not be available otherwise. Members of the public pulled letters out of attics and uploaded them to the system, where they joined the records that institutions have sanctified as canonical history. The network visualisations that interpret the project show the result: a picture of history that connects voices at the periphery to those already at the centre. (One contributor wrote, “I found it very moving that Uncle Wesley’s letter had been accepted as a genuine and valued fragment of Ireland’s history. It was almost as if I had been accepted as Irish for the first time.”[Footnote26](#)) Indeed, Schreibman, Das Gupta and Rooney suggest that the project’s success owes much to its exploitation of the commerce between the physical and the digital world. The digital is physical, as technologies remind us at their physical limits: network cables break, disks corrupt, laptops overheat and smell of ozone. The bodily practices that attended the work of volunteers—transcribing, for example, which Schreibman, Das Gupta and Rooney describe as a more intimate form of engagement than simply reading, a way of chewing the Host of the word—turned memory into the act of memory, sustaining their motivation by nurturing a sense of intimacy with the past.

Data visualisation often serves as a tool for business analytics, and Letters of 1916 used it, in part, for a similar purpose: as a tool for understanding users and thus better fostering user participation. The project’s directors gathered qualitative data from a user survey as well as quantitative data about user activity from the project’s database. They concluded that the motivations of their users cannot be understood according to the rubric of a *gift economy* that has dominated theories about the web since its beginning.[Footnote27](#) Rather, the data showed a pattern of user activity that insists on, not the gift, but the regular performance of giving; visualisations of user activity show a regular rise and fall across the day and week. Drawing on Paul Connerton’s work on social memory, they connect the acts of memory that such performances entail with the symbolic and bodily practices that groups have always used to remember: “The almost ritualistic adherence to the transcription work displays a community that is invested in the resource as a part of daily life.”[Footnote28](#) (In keeping with Connerton’s emphasis on ceremonial action, the user surveys showed that the ceremonial value of the anniversary year of 2016 helped to attract user participation.) They used this data to time social media campaigns and inform public outreach. In short, the practical challenges of building and running a public engagement project informed the theoretical work on social memory that the project’s epistolary focus elicited.

In her meta-theoretical essay, Urszula Pawlicka, who has written extensively about the spaces and infrastructures of the digital humanities, adopts a view from 30,000 feet to survey the forces that have given rise to the kind of humanities work that the other essays discuss. She argues that our adoption of data analysis and visualisation, which support practice-based, project-oriented, collaborative research with utilitarian goals, is less a development arising from new technologies than a response from within humanities departments to “neo-economic” forces that have driven humanities departments to a point of crisis. For her, these practices represent “a response to the external conditions that surround the humanities rather than an internal conviction that arises from work within our disciplines”—a “suite of goals” for which she proposes the umbrella term “tactical humanities”.[Footnote29](#) Her account will surely be of equal value for those who seek to defend the humanities against what Silicon Valley calls “disruption” and the unrepentant tactical humanists who seek the opposite. Pawlicka reminds us that concepts entail actions, that tools trail clouds of intent from the fields in which they were designed, and that the models we borrow from other fields may smuggle in foreign tenets:

If, as Bruno Latour claims, the laboratory is an ordinary rather than a “scientific” place, and yet a place that has been socially constructed so as to project the mythos of science onto the world outside its walls, then humanities laboratories may begin by borrowing the foreign glamour of science and end by reproducing its ideological parochialisms.[Footnote30](#)

Finally, in an interview, Andrew Piper and Mark Algee-Hewitt discuss the uses, in their pioneering project, The Werther Effect, of data visualisation as an exploratory tool, a form of “reverse ekphrasis” that can render the linear multidimensional and the successive simultaneous, and a negotiative language for scholars working across zones of expertise—across different ways of modelling, representing, knowing—in a computer-age version of what Lorraine Daston and Peter Galison have called “four-eyed sight”.[Footnote31](#) A study of influence via semantic dissemination, or rather a study that “model[s] the novel as a semantic distribution, and how that distribution brings other clusters of texts together”,[Footnote32](#) The Werther Effect charts the paths, in the later work of Goethe and in the larger European corpus, of the semantic particularities of Goethe’s 1774 novel, *The Sorrows of Young Werther*. The project’s implementation of a model of visualisation that is non-intuitive for the study of effects across metrics other than space—network topology, articulated via Voronoi diagrams—identified and quantified relationships among texts that literary study’s conventional vantages could not have seen. It also offered a model of distant reading that does not stop at the level of algorithmic reading, but rather establishes sites of interest for close reading—“portals” to the archive. “With topology, we reduce the problem to a scalar problem, so that reading happens at all scales,” says Algee-Hewitt. “Topology lets us see at a distance and also come closer. We look at maps of Goethe’s texts; then we look at individual pages.”[Footnote33](#)

Taken together, the voices in these pages articulate a range of ways in which specifically literary sensibilities can inform or interrogate the visual analysis of data. This special issue aims to participate in a field-specific reckoning of the methods and meanings of data visualisation. But it aims also to engage in outreach on behalf of visualisation as a tool to a journal readership that includes many self-described “traditional” humanists, and to share in a tradition of internal scrutiny that runs through every representational system. enriched_points.expanded_content: Data visualization enhances the visibility of humanities research, allowing scholars to present complex findings in accessible formats, reaching broader audiences beyond traditional academic circles.

Visualization tools foster interdisciplinary collaboration by enabling scholars from diverse fields to share insights, methodologies, and data, creating a richer research environment and innovative projects.

The use of digital methods prompts scholars to explore previously unconsidered questions, shifting focus from qualitative analysis to quantitative patterns, enriching humanities scholarship and expanding inquiry.
